content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_e072b769

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains multiple durable personal preferences/habits and past experiences plus broader reusable context about grocery planning, sustainability, and cashback strategy, so mixed memory is appropriate under force_memory_write.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving the stable namespace-level user; no existing user entity is available.
- mention=Ibotta; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=SERVICE; name=Ibotta; reason=Ibotta is a concrete reusable service discussed throughout the session; no existing entity is available.
- mention=reusable bags; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's reusable bags; reason=First-person possessive/context indicates concrete personal items that later claims may reference.
- mention=Costco; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Costco; reason=Costco is a concrete retailer mentioned as a place for bulk shopping; no existing entity is available.
- mention=FreshToDoor; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=SERVICE; name=FreshToDoor; reason=FreshToDoor is a named grocery delivery service the user previously tried; no existing entity is available.
- mention=Thrive Market; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=SERVICE; name=Thrive Market; reason=Thrive Market is a concrete reusable online grocery service discussed as a target for purchases; no existing entity is available.
- mention=Instacart; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=SERVICE; name=Instacart; reason=Instacart is a named grocery delivery service recommended as an alternative; no existing entity is available.
- mention=Shipt; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=SERVICE; name=Shipt; reason=Shipt is a named grocery delivery service recommended as an alternative; no existing entity is available.
- mention=Peapod; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=SERVICE; name=Peapod; reason=Peapod is a named grocery service recommended as an alternative; no existing entity is available.
- mention=Google Express; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=SERVICE; name=Google Express; reason=Google Express is a named delivery service recommended as an alternative; no existing entity is available.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Ibotta|Thrive Market|FreshToDoor|Costco|reusable bags
        text_queries=grocery shopping household size dietary restrictions Ibotta eggs rebate cashback|reusable bags habit reduce plastic waste bulk shopping Costco|FreshToDoor Thrive Market grocery delivery first-time promotions organic natural products
        predicate_hints=has_constraint|prefers|has_goal|has_experience_with|works_with

Session date: 2023/04/16 (Sun) 04:50
Transcript:
user: I'm trying to plan my next grocery trip and I was wondering if you could help me make a list of essentials I should pick up. I've just downloaded Ibotta, a cashback app that gives you rebates on certain grocery items, and I want to make sure I get the most out of it.
assistant: I'd be happy to help you plan your grocery trip and maximize your Ibotta earnings.

To get started, I'll need to know a bit more about your dietary preferences and the types of products you typically buy. Please answer the following questions:

1. Do you have any dietary restrictions or preferences (e.g., vegetarian, gluten-free, dairy-free, keto)?
2. What is your household size?
3. What types of groceries do you usually buy (e.g., fresh produce, meat, dairy, canned goods, snacks, baking supplies)?
4. Do you have a preferred store or chain where you shop?
5. Are there any specific Ibotta offers that you've been eyeing or want to prioritize?

Once I have this information, I can provide you with a list of essential items that are commonly found on Ibotta and tailored to your needs.

Additionally, here are some general tips to maximize your Ibotta earnings:

* Check the Ibotta app regularly for new offers and expiring deals.
* Plan your shopping trip around the items with the highest cashback percentages or values.
* Buy in bulk if possible, especially for non-perishable items.
* Consider shopping during sales or using digital coupons to stack your savings.
* Always read the offer details and terms to ensure you understand what's required to earn the cashback.

Let me know your answers to the above questions, and I'll help you create a personalized grocery list to get the most out of Ibotta!
user: I don't have any dietary restrictions or preferences, and I'm the only one in my household. I usually buy a mix of everything, like fresh produce, meat, dairy, canned goods, and snacks. I don't have a preferred store, but I do try to shop at places that offer cashback through Ibotta. I've been eyeing that rebate on eggs, so I'll probably prioritize that.
assistant: Based on your preferences, I've compiled a list of essential grocery items that are commonly found on Ibotta and should give you a good starting point for your next shopping trip. I've also included some tips to help you maximize your cashback earnings:

**Produce:**

1. Fresh fruits (e.g., apples, bananas, berries)
2. Fresh vegetables (e.g., carrots, broccoli, bell peppers)
3. Salad mix or greens
4. Herbs (e.g., basil, cilantro, parsley)

**Dairy and Eggs:**

1. Eggs (prioritize this one for the rebate!)
2. Milk (gallon or half-gallon)
3. Yogurt (individual cups or a large container)
4. Cheese (shredded, block, or string)

**Meat and Protein:**

1. Boneless, skinless chicken breasts
2. Ground beef or ground turkey
3. Salmon or other frozen fish fillets
4. Tofu or other protein alternatives

**Canned Goods:**

1. Canned black beans or kidney beans
2. Diced tomatoes
3. Canned tuna or chicken
4. Soups (e.g., tomato soup, black bean soup)

**Snacks:**

1. Granola or energy bars
2. Nuts (e.g., almonds, walnuts)
3. Dried fruits (e.g., cranberries, raisins)
4. Crackers or chips

**Pantry Items:**

1. Bread (loaf or buns)
2. Pasta or rice
3. Oils (e.g., olive, coconut)
4. Spices (e.g., salt, pepper, basil)

**Beverages:**

1. Juice (e.g., orange, apple)
2. Coffee or tea
3. Water (bottled or filtered)
4. Soda or sparkling water

**Tips to Maximize Ibotta Earnings:**

1. Check Ibotta for offers on the items you need before you shop.
2. Buy in bulk if possible, especially for non-perishable items.
3. Look for "any brand" or "any item" offers to maximize your cashback.
4. Stack your savings by using digital coupons or shopping during sales.
5. Keep an eye on the Ibotta app for bonus offers or special deals.

By focusing on these essentials, you'll be able to stock your pantry and fridge while earning cashback through Ibotta. Don't forget to prioritize that egg rebate!
user: I'm also thinking of trying to reduce my plastic usage, so do you think I could get some reusable bags or containers that would be suitable for grocery shopping?
assistant: That's a great idea! Reducing plastic usage is a wonderful step towards a more sustainable lifestyle. Reusable bags and containers can make a significant impact, and they're often eligible for cashback through Ibotta as well!

Here are some eco-friendly options you might consider:

**Reusable Bags:**

1. Tote bags: Look for sturdy, washable totes made from cotton, canvas, or recycled materials. They're perfect for carrying groceries, and many stores offer discounts for using reusable bags.
2. Produce bags: These are lightweight, mesh bags designed for carrying fruits and vegetables. They're usually made from breathable materials and can be washed and reused multiple times.

**Reusable Containers:**

1. Glass or stainless steel containers: These are great for storing and transporting items like meat, dairy, or snacks. Look for containers with secure lids and a durable design.
2. Beeswax wraps: These are a sustainable alternative to plastic wrap for covering bowls or wrapping snacks. They're made from natural beeswax, jojoba oil, and cotton.

**Tips for Using Reusable Bags and Containers:**

1. Keep a few reusable bags in your car or by the door so you always have them handy.
2. Designate specific containers for specific items (e.g., meat, dairy, snacks) to avoid cross-contamination.
3. Clean and sanitize your reusable containers regularly to prevent bacterial growth.
4. Consider purchasing reusable bags or containers with a brand that offers a discount or cashback through Ibotta.

Some popular brands that offer eco-friendly reusable bags and containers include:

* BagPodz
* Bee's Wrap
* Stasher Bag
* S'well
* LunchBots

Before making a purchase, check Ibotta for any available offers or rebates on these brands or products. You might be able to earn cashback on your eco-friendly purchases!

Remember, every small change you make can add up to make a big difference in reducing plastic waste. Kudos to you for taking this step towards a more sustainable lifestyle!
user: I've been carrying reusable bags with me whenever I go shopping, and it's become a habit now. I feel good about not contributing to the plastic waste. Do you think I could also use these reusable bags for bulk shopping at places like Costco?
assistant: That's awesome to hear that you've made reusable bags a habit!

Yes, you can definitely use your reusable bags for bulk shopping at places like Costco! In fact, many bulk stores encourage customers to bring their own bags or containers to reduce waste. Here are some tips to keep in mind:

**Using Reusable Bags for Bulk Shopping:**

1. **Check store policies:** Before you head to Costco or any other bulk store, check their website or call ahead to confirm their policy on using reusable bags for bulk shopping. Some stores might have specific guidelines or restrictions.
2. **Choose the right bags:** Select sturdy, larger reusable bags that can hold a decent amount of weight and are easy to carry. You might consider bags with reinforced handles or a flat bottom for easier loading.
3. **Pack wisely:** When filling your reusable bags, make sure to pack items carefully to avoid damage or spills. You might consider using smaller containers or bags within your larger reusable bag to organize items and prevent messes.
4. **Be mindful of weight:** Be aware of the weight capacity of your reusable bags and don't overload them. You don't want to risk damaging the bag or straining your back!
5. **Clean your bags:** After each use, make sure to clean and sanitize your reusable bags to prevent cross-contamination and keep them in good condition.

**Additional Tips for Bulk Shopping with Reusable Bags:**

1. **Bring smaller bags for smaller items:** Consider bringing smaller reusable bags or containers for items like nuts, grains, or spices to make it easier to scoop and pour.
2. **Use the store's scales:** If you're buying items like coffee or nuts in bulk, use the store's scales to weigh your items before pouring them into your reusable bags.
3. **Be prepared for cashier questions:** Some cashiers might ask you to remove items from your reusable bags for scanning or weighing. Be patient and cooperative, and don't hesitate to ask for help if needed.

By using your reusable bags for bulk shopping, you'll not only reduce waste but also save money on single-use bags and potentially earn cashback through Ibotta!

Keep up the great work, and happy bulk shopping!
user: I was thinking of trying out a new grocery delivery service, something similar to FreshToDoor, which I tried a few weeks ago. Do you know of any other services that offer similar promotions or discounts, especially for first-time users?
assistant: Grocery delivery services can be super convenient, and it's great that you're exploring options.

Yes, there are several grocery delivery services that offer promotions and discounts, especially for first-time users. Here are a few options you might want to consider:

1. **Instacart:** Instacart is a popular grocery delivery service that partners with local stores to deliver fresh produce, meat, dairy, and other essentials. They often offer promotions for first-time users, such as $10 off your first order or free delivery on your first purchase.
2. **Shipt:** Shipt is a membership-based grocery delivery service that offers unlimited free delivery on orders over $35. They often provide discounts for new members, such as $15 off your first order or a free 2-week trial.
3. **Peapod:** Peapod is an online grocery store that offers delivery or pickup options. They frequently offer promotions for new customers, such as $20 off your first order or 10% off your first purchase.
4. **Google Express:** Google Express is a delivery service that partners with local stores to deliver groceries, household essentials, and more. They sometimes offer discounts for new users, such as $15 off your first order or free delivery on your first purchase.
5. **Thrive Market:** Thrive Market is an online grocery store that specializes in organic and natural products. They often provide discounts for new customers, such as 25% off your first order or a free 30-day trial.

When signing up for a new service, be sure to check their website or app for any available promotions or discounts. You can also try using promo codes or referral links from friends or family members to get a discount on your first order.

Additionally, keep an eye on your Ibotta app, as they sometimes offer cashback on grocery delivery services or online grocery purchases.

Remember to read reviews, check the service's availability in your area, and review their pricing and delivery fees before committing to a particular service. Happy shopping!
user: I'm thinking of trying out Thrive Market, as I've been trying to incorporate more organic and natural products into my diet. Do you think I could get cashback on my Thrive Market purchases through Ibotta?
assistant: Thrive Market is a fantastic platform for accessing organic and natural products.

As for cashback on Thrive Market purchases through Ibotta, the answer is... YES! Ibotta has partnered with Thrive Market to offer cashback on certain purchases.

Here's how it works:

1. **Link your Thrive Market account:** Make sure you have an account with Thrive Market and link it to your Ibotta account. You can do this by going to the Ibotta app, searching for Thrive Market, and following the prompts to link your accounts.
2. **Browse Thrive Market offers:** Check the Ibotta app for available cashback offers on Thrive Market purchases. These offers might be on specific products, categories, or even entire purchases.
3. **Shop on Thrive Market:** Head to Thrive Market's website or app and make a purchase. Make sure to meet the requirements for the cashback offer you want to redeem.
4. **Get cashback:** After your purchase, Ibotta will credit your account with the cashback amount. You can then redeem your cashback rewards for real money or use them to make future purchases.

Some tips to maximize your cashback on Thrive Market purchases:

* **Check Ibotta regularly:** Offers on Thrive Market products can change frequently, so make sure to check the Ibotta app regularly for new deals.
* **Stack your savings:** Look for sales or discounts on Thrive Market and combine them with Ibotta cashback offers to maximize your savings.
* **Read the fine print:** Always review the terms and conditions of each cashback offer to ensure you understand the requirements and any exclusions.

By shopping on Thrive Market through Ibotta, you can earn cashback on your organic and natural product purchases, making your healthy choices even more rewarding!

Happy shopping, and enjoy your Thrive Market experience!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_e072b769

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains multiple durable personal preferences/habits and past experiences plus broader reusable context about grocery planning, sustainability, and cashback strategy, so mixed memory is appropriate under force_memory_write.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm trying to plan my next grocery trip and I was wondering if you could help me make a list of essentials I should pick up. I've just downloaded Ibotta, a cashback app that gives you rebates on certain grocery items, and I want to make sure I get the most out of it.
assistant: I'd be happy to help you plan your grocery trip and maximize your Ibotta earnings.

To get started, I'll need to know a bit more about your dietary preferences and the types of products you typically buy. Please answer the following questions:

1. Do you have any dietary restrictions or preferences (e.g., vegetarian, gluten-free, dairy-free, keto)?
2. What is your household size?
3. What types of groceries do you usually buy (e.g., fresh produce, meat, dairy, canned goods, snacks, baking supplies)?
4. Do you have a preferred store or chain where you shop?
5. Are there any specific Ibotta offers that you've been eyeing or want to prioritize?

Once I have this information, I can provide you with a list of essential items that are commonly found on Ibotta and tailored to your needs.

Additionally, here are some general tips to maximize your Ibotta earnings:

* Check the Ibotta app regularly for new offers and expiring deals.
* Plan your shopping trip around the items with the highest cashback percentages or values.
* Buy in bulk if possible, especially for non-perishable items.
* Consider shopping during sales or using digital coupons to stack your savings.
* Always read the offer details and terms to ensure you understand what's required to earn the cashback.

Let me know your answers to the above questions, and I'll help you create a personalized grocery list to get the most out of Ibotta!
user: I don't have any dietary restrictions or preferences, and I'm the only one in my household. I usually buy a mix of everything, like fresh produce, meat, dairy, canned goods, and snacks. I don't have a preferred store, but I do try to shop at places that offer cashback through Ibotta. I've been eyeing that rebate on eggs, so I'll probably prioritize that.
assistant: Based on your preferences, I've compiled a list of essential grocery items that are commonly found on Ibotta and should give you a good starting point for your next shopping trip. I've also included some tips to help you maximize your cashback earnings:

**Produce:**

1. Fresh fruits (e.g., apples, bananas, berries)
2. Fresh vegetables (e.g., carrots, broccoli, bell peppers)
3. Salad mix or greens
4. Herbs (e.g., basil, cilantro, parsley)

**Dairy and Eggs:**

1. Eggs (prioritize this one for the rebate!)
2. Milk (gallon or half-gallon)
3. Yogurt (individual cups or a large container)
4. Cheese (shredded, block, or string)

**Meat and Protein:**

1. Boneless, skinless chicken breasts
2. Ground beef or ground turkey
3. Salmon or other frozen fish fillets
4. Tofu or other protein alternatives

**Canned Goods:**

1. Canned black beans or kidney beans
2. Diced tomatoes
3. Canned tuna or chicken
4. Soups (e.g., tomato soup, black bean soup)

**Snacks:**

1. Granola or energy bars
2. Nuts (e.g., almonds, walnuts)
3. Dried fruits (e.g., cranberries, raisins)
4. Crackers or chips

**Pantry Items:**

1. Bread (loaf or buns)
2. Pasta or rice
3. Oils (e.g., olive, coconut)
4. Spices (e.g., salt, pepper, basil)

**Beverages:**

1. Juice (e.g., orange, apple)
2. Coffee or tea
3. Water (bottled or filtered)
4. Soda or sparkling water

**Tips to Maximize Ibotta Earnings:**

1. Check Ibotta for offers on the items you need before you shop.
2. Buy in bulk if possible, especially for non-perishable items.
3. Look for "any brand" or "any item" offers to maximize your cashback.
4. Stack your savings by using digital coupons or shopping during sales.
5. Keep an eye on the Ibotta app for bonus offers or special deals.

By focusing on these essentials, you'll be able to stock your pantry and fridge while earning cashback through Ibotta. Don't forget to prioritize that egg rebate!
user: I'm also thinking of trying to reduce my plastic usage, so do you think I could get some reusable bags or containers that would be suitable for grocery shopping?
assistant: That's a great idea! Reducing plastic usage is a wonderful step towards a more sustainable lifestyle. Reusable bags and containers can make a significant impact, and they're often eligible for cashback through Ibotta as well!

Here are some eco-friendly options you might consider:

**Reusable Bags:**

1. Tote bags: Look for sturdy, washable totes made from cotton, canvas, or recycled materials. They're perfect for carrying groceries, and many stores offer discounts for using reusable bags.
2. Produce bags: These are lightweight, mesh bags designed for carrying fruits and vegetables. They're usually made from breathable materials and can be washed and reused multiple times.

**Reusable Containers:**

1. Glass or stainless steel containers: These are great for storing and transporting items like meat, dairy, or snacks. Look for containers with secure lids and a durable design.
2. Beeswax wraps: These are a sustainable alternative to plastic wrap for covering bowls or wrapping snacks. They're made from natural beeswax, jojoba oil, and cotton.

**Tips for Using Reusable Bags and Containers:**

1. Keep a few reusable bags in your car or by the door so you always have them handy.
2. Designate specific containers for specific items (e.g., meat, dairy, snacks) to avoid cross-contamination.
3. Clean and sanitize your reusable containers regularly to prevent bacterial growth.
4. Consider purchasing reusable bags or containers with a brand that offers a discount or cashback through Ibotta.

Some popular brands that offer eco-friendly reusable bags and containers include:

* BagPodz
* Bee's Wrap
* Stasher Bag
* S'well
* LunchBots

Before making a purchase, check Ibotta for any available offers or rebates on these brands or products. You might be able to earn cashback on your eco-friendly purchases!

Remember, every small change you make can add up to make a big difference in reducing plastic waste. Kudos to you for taking this step towards a more sustainable lifestyle!
user: I've been carrying reusable bags with me whenever I go shopping, and it's become a habit now. I feel good about not contributing to the plastic waste. Do you think I could also use these reusable bags for bulk shopping at places like Costco?
assistant: That's awesome to hear that you've made reusable bags a habit!

Yes, you can definitely use your reusable bags for bulk shopping at places like Costco! In fact, many bulk stores encourage customers to bring their own bags or containers to reduce waste. Here are some tips to keep in mind:

**Using Reusable Bags for Bulk Shopping:**

1. **Check store policies:** Before you head to Costco or any other bulk store, check their website or call ahead to confirm their policy on using reusable bags for bulk shopping. Some stores might have specific guidelines or restrictions.
2. **Choose the right bags:** Select sturdy, larger reusable bags that can hold a decent amount of weight and are easy to carry. You might consider bags with reinforced handles or a flat bottom for easier loading.
3. **Pack wisely:** When filling your reusable bags, make sure to pack items carefully to avoid damage or spills. You might consider using smaller containers or bags within your larger reusable bag to organize items and prevent messes.
4. **Be mindful of weight:** Be aware of the weight capacity of your reusable bags and don't overload them. You don't want to risk damaging the bag or straining your back!
5. **Clean your bags:** After each use, make sure to clean and sanitize your reusable bags to prevent cross-contamination and keep them in good condition.

**Additional Tips for Bulk Shopping with Reusable Bags:**

1. **Bring smaller bags for smaller items:** Consider bringing smaller reusable bags or containers for items like nuts, grains, or spices to make it easier to scoop and pour.
2. **Use the store's scales:** If you're buying items like coffee or nuts in bulk, use the store's scales to weigh your items before pouring them into your reusable bags.
3. **Be prepared for cashier questions:** Some cashiers might ask you to remove items from your reusable bags for scanning or weighing. Be patient and cooperative, and don't hesitate to ask for help if needed.

By using your reusable bags for bulk shopping, you'll not only reduce waste but also save money on single-use bags and potentially earn cashback through Ibotta!

Keep up the great work, and happy bulk shopping!
user: I was thinking of trying out a new grocery delivery service, something similar to FreshToDoor, which I tried a few weeks ago. Do you know of any other services that offer similar promotions or discounts, especially for first-time users?
assistant: Grocery delivery services can be super convenient, and it's great that you're exploring options.

Yes, there are several grocery delivery services that offer promotions and discounts, especially for first-time users. Here are a few options you might want to consider:

1. **Instacart:** Instacart is a popular grocery delivery service that partners with local stores to deliver fresh produce, meat, dairy, and other essentials. They often offer promotions for first-time users, such as $10 off your first order or free delivery on your first purchase.
2. **Shipt:** Shipt is a membership-based grocery delivery service that offers unlimited free delivery on orders over $35. They often provide discounts for new members, such as $15 off your first order or a free 2-week trial.
3. **Peapod:** Peapod is an online grocery store that offers delivery or pickup options. They frequently offer promotions for new customers, such as $20 off your first order or 10% off your first purchase.
4. **Google Express:** Google Express is a delivery service that partners with local stores to deliver groceries, household essentials, and more. They sometimes offer discounts for new users, such as $15 off your first order or free delivery on your first purchase.
5. **Thrive Market:** Thrive Market is an online grocery store that specializes in organic and natural products. They often provide discounts for new customers, such as 25% off your first order or a free 30-day trial.

When signing up for a new service, be sure to check their website or app for any available promotions or discounts. You can also try using promo codes or referral links from friends or family members to get a discount on your first order.

Additionally, keep an eye on your Ibotta app, as they sometimes offer cashback on grocery delivery services or online grocery purchases.

Remember to read reviews, check the service's availability in your area, and review their pricing and delivery fees before committing to a particular service. Happy shopping!
user: I'm thinking of trying out Thrive Market, as I've been trying to incorporate more organic and natural products into my diet. Do you think I could get cashback on my Thrive Market purchases through Ibotta?
assistant: Thrive Market is a fantastic platform for accessing organic and natural products.

As for cashback on Thrive Market purchases through Ibotta, the answer is... YES! Ibotta has partnered with Thrive Market to offer cashback on certain purchases.

Here's how it works:

1. **Link your Thrive Market account:** Make sure you have an account with Thrive Market and link it to your Ibotta account. You can do this by going to the Ibotta app, searching for Thrive Market, and following the prompts to link your accounts.
2. **Browse Thrive Market offers:** Check the Ibotta app for available cashback offers on Thrive Market purchases. These offers might be on specific products, categories, or even entire purchases.
3. **Shop on Thrive Market:** Head to Thrive Market's website or app and make a purchase. Make sure to meet the requirements for the cashback offer you want to redeem.
4. **Get cashback:** After your purchase, Ibotta will credit your account with the cashback amount. You can then redeem your cashback rewards for real money or use them to make future purchases.

Some tips to maximize your cashback on Thrive Market purchases:

* **Check Ibotta regularly:** Offers on Thrive Market products can change frequently, so make sure to check the Ibotta app regularly for new deals.
* **Stack your savings:** Look for sales or discounts on Thrive Market and combine them with Ibotta cashback offers to maximize your savings.
* **Read the fine print:** Always review the terms and conditions of each cashback offer to ensure you understand the requirements and any exclusions.

By shopping on Thrive Market through Ibotta, you can earn cashback on your organic and natural product purchases, making your healthy choices even more rewarding!

Happy shopping, and enjoy your Thrive Market experience!

Their distinguishing promotion angles were first-order discounts or free delivery/trials, with Thrive Market standing out for organic and natural products and possible 25% off or a free 30-day trial for new customers.","tags":["grocery_delivery","promotions","comparison"],"title":"Grocery delivery alternatives and first-time promotion options","valid_from":"2023-04-16T04:50:00Z","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["interested in Thrive Market for organic and natural products","asked whether Ibotta cashback applies to Thrive Market"],"confidence":0.88,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<5>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"secondary"}],"evidence_kind":"summarized","evidence_quote":"Make sure you have an account with Thrive Market and link it to your Ibotta account.","evidence_reason":"The assistant provided an explicit step-by-step cashback workflow for Thrive Market through Ibotta in response to the user's intent.","importance":3,"keywords":["Thrive Market","Ibotta","cashback","link account","organic products","stack savings","offer requirements"],"note_type":"plan","rationale":"This is a procedure/workflow and savings plan; it is not a simple durable factual claim, especially because offer availability can change.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"Planned approach for using Ibotta cashback with Thrive Market purchases"},"summary":"The discussed workflow for earning Ibotta cashback on Thrive Market purchases was to link the Thrive Market account in Ibotta, browse current Thrive Market offers, shop through Thrive Market while meeting offer requirements, and then receive Ibotta credit.
